# Summative challenge: coral cover, logging & random effects

This brings the whole day together. You will build and fit a model that combines
**everything**: count-like data, a log link, a continuous predictor, a factor,
and random effects for grouping.

## The scenario

Same Kia Province case study as the fish counts (Module 14), but now we look at
the habitat itself. On each reef, divers ran several transects and recorded the
per-cent cover of branching *Acropora* coral — the nursery habitat the juvenile
bumphead parrotfish depend on. The worry is the same: terrestrial logging washes
sediment onto the reef and smothers the branching coral, so cover should be
lower closer to logging.

`random-effects.csv` has one row per transect:

- `cover` — per-cent cover of branching *Acropora* (our response).
- `dist_to_logging_km` — the site's distance from the nearest logging operation.
- `flow` — water flow, a two-level factor (`Strong`, `Mild`); FANGS encodes it in order of first appearance, so `Strong` = 1 and `Mild` = 2.
- `site` — which reef the transect belongs to. Because there are multiple transects per site, transects from the same reef are correlated, so `site` is our random-effects grouping variable.

> **Data & attribution.** From the same study as Module 14: Hamilton, R.J.,
> Almany, G.R., Brown, C.J., Pita, J., Peterson, N.A. & Choat, J.H. (2017)
> *Logging degrades nursery habitat for an iconic coral reef fish.*
> **Biological Conservation** 210: 273–280.
> <https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biocon.2017.04.025>. Lightly modified for teaching
> with FANGS.

## The model

We treat per-cent cover as a count on the log scale, add the distance and flow
effects, and let each site have its own random intercept to absorb
transect-to-transect clustering:

$$
\text{cover}_i \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda_i), \qquad \log(\lambda_i) = \alpha + \beta\,\text{dist}_i + \beta_{\text{flow}}\,(\text{flow}_i - 1) + b_{\text{site}[i]}, \qquad b_j \sim \text{Normal}(0, \sigma_b)
$$

```
model {
  for (i in 1:N) {
    cover[i] ~ dpois(lambda[i])
    log(lambda[i]) <- alpha + beta * dist_to_logging_km[i] + beta_flow * (flow[i] - 1) + b[site[i]]
  }
  for (j in 1:J) {
    b[j] ~ dnorm(0, sigma.b)
  }
  alpha     ~ dnorm(0, 10)
  beta      ~ dnorm(0, 5)
  beta_flow ~ dnorm(0, 5)
  sigma.b   ~ dunif(0, 10)
}
```

Set `N` and `J` (the number of sites, 49) in the constants.

## Bring your whole toolkit

- Set sensible, weakly informative priors, and avoid the over-specification trap from Module 20.
- **Run a prior predictive check** before fitting (Module 7).
- After fitting, check **R-hat and ESS** (Module 10) — repair the model if the variance parameters mix badly.
- Run a **posterior predictive check** (Module 9) to confirm the model reproduces the cover values.
- Interpret the distance and flow effects multiplicatively ($e^\beta$), exactly as in Modules 14–15.

## Your task

Fit the full model to `random-effects.csv` and read the **Summary** tab for
$\alpha$, the distance slope $\beta$, the flow effect $\beta_{\text{flow}}$, and
the site SD $\sigma_b$. Does cover rise with distance from logging, as the
sediment-damage story predicts? Pass this and you have fit a real hierarchical
GLM end to end. 🎉
